title: Display & Edit Methods in X++
summary: Master display and edit methods to create calculated fields, custom UI behaviors, and data transformations in D365 F&O forms and reports.

content.overview:
Display and edit methods are one of X++'s most powerful features for creating calculated fields and custom UI behaviors without modifying base application tables. These methods execute on-demand when forms render or reports generate, allowing you to present transformed data, perform calculations, or control field editability based on runtime conditions. Unlike stored database fields, display/edit methods are computed in real-time, making them perfect for derived values, status indicators, and conditional formatting.

In D365 F&O architecture, display methods are read-only calculated fields that return values for display purposes, while edit methods provide two-way data binding for complex field interactions. They're essential for customizations that need to present data differently than it's stored, implement business logic for field validation, or create composite fields that combine multiple table fields. Understanding their execution context, caching behavior, and performance implications is crucial for building responsive, maintainable solutions.
content.theory:
Display and edit methods are declared with the [Display] and [Edit] attributes respectively, and must follow specific naming and signature conventions. The framework automatically discovers these methods through reflection and binds them to form controls or report fields based on the Extended Data Type (EDT) specified in the attribute. The method execution occurs within the table's context, giving you access to all table fields and methods through the 'this' reference.

The caching mechanism is critical to understand: display methods are cached per record until the record's data changes or the cache is explicitly invalidated using the table's reread() method. This prevents redundant calculations but can cause stale data if external dependencies change. The SysTableBrowser class and form data sources handle this caching automatically, but custom scenarios may require manual cache management.

For form integration, display/edit methods bound to form controls automatically participate in the form's data binding lifecycle. The FormDataSource calls these methods during record navigation, refresh operations, and field validation. Edit methods receive the new value as a parameter and must handle validation, transformation, and persistence to underlying table fields. They should call the table's validateField() method to trigger standard validation frameworks.

code_examples.title: Basic Display Method with Business Logic
code_examples.description: A display method on CustTable that calculates customer credit utilization percentage with proper error handling and null checking

code_examples.title: Advanced Edit Method with Validation
code_examples.description: An edit method that handles complex field interactions, validation, and automatic field updates when dependencies change

code_examples.title: Performance-Optimized Display Method with Caching
code_examples.description: A complex display method that implements custom caching and batch data retrieval to avoid N+1 query problems

key_points: Display methods are read-only and execute on-demand — they should never modify table data directly, only return calculated or transformed values for presentation
Edit methods provide bidirectional data binding — the boolean parameter indicates if it's a get (_set=false) or set (_set=true) operation, and you must handle both scenarios
Method signatures must match exact patterns — display methods return the EDT type and take no parameters, edit methods return EDT type and take boolean + value parameters
Caching behavior is automatic but controllable — the framework caches results per record until data changes, but you can force refresh with table.reread() or implement custom caching
Performance implications are significant — display methods execute for every visible record in grids, so optimize queries and consider batch loading for expensive operations
gotchas: Display methods in grids execute for EVERY visible row — if your grid shows 50 records and your display method does a database query, that's 50 queries. Use static caching or preload data to avoid N+1 problems
Edit method validation exceptions don't automatically rollback — wrap database modifications in ttsbegin/ttscommit blocks and handle rollback explicitly in catch blocks to maintain data consistency

Recursive display method calls cause stack overflow — if display method A calls a method that triggers display method B, which calls back to A, you'll crash. Use static flags or redesign the dependency chain
performance_tips: Implement static caching for expensive display methods that show the same data across multiple records — use Maps with cache expiry logic to balance performance and data freshness
Use batch preloading in form initialization — call a static method that loads all required data in a single query, then cache results for individual display method calls

quiz.q: What happens when a display method is bound to a grid column showing 100 records?
options: The method executes once and the result is shared
The method executes 100 times, once per record
The method executes only for visible rows on screen
The method is cached and executes only when data changes
quiz.explanation: Display methods execute once for each record in the grid, which can cause performance issues. This is why caching and batch preloading are crucial for expensive operations.

quiz.q: In an edit method, how do you determine if you should return the current value or set a new value?
options: Check if the second parameter is null
Check if the method was called from a form
quiz.explanation: Edit methods use the boolean _set parameter to determine the operation mode: false means get/return current value, true means set/validate the new value provided in the second parameter.

quiz.q: What is the best way to handle database queries in display methods for optimal performance?
options: Use direct SQL queries instead of X++ select statements
Implement static caching with expiry logic
Always use firstonly in select statements
Call the display method recursively to build results
quiz.explanation: Static caching with expiry logic is the best approach because it prevents repeated database queries for the same data while ensuring data freshness through cache invalidation strategies.
